acesulfame potassium.desc: Zero-calorie sweetener. CSPI advises caution due to potential thyroid effects in animal studies.

allura red.desc: Red 40 (E129). CSPI report links it to behavioral issues in children. Most common food dye.

ammonium carbonate.desc: E503. Industrial leavening agent permitted by FSSAI.

artificial color.desc: Undisclosed synthetic dyes. FSSAI requires specific declaration to avoid hidden risks.

artificial flavouring substances.desc: Synthetic mixtures. FSSAI distinguishes them from natural flavors. Drivers of hyper-palatability.

artificial flavours.desc: Identified as medium risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

aspartame.desc: Classified by IARC (WHO) as 'Possibly Carcinogenic to Humans' (Group 2B). Also linked to headaches.

baking powder.desc: Chemical leavener. Often contains sodium aluminium sulfate (neurotoxicity concerns).

bha.desc: E320. NIH lists it as 'Reasonably Anticipated to be a Human Carcinogen'.

bht.desc: E321. Restricted in EU due to potential endocrine disrupting effects.

brilliant blue.desc: Blue 1 (E133). Coal-tar derivative. FDA approved but concerns exist regarding hypersensitivity.

canola oil.desc: Extracted using hexane. While low in sat fat, the refining process removes antioxidants (Vitamin E).

carboxymethyl cellulose.desc: CMC (E466). Clinical trial in Gastroenterology (2021) showed it alters gut microbiome diversity.

carrageenan.desc: E407. JECFA reviewed. Degraded carrageenan is unsafe; food-grade is controversial for gut inflammation.

citric acid.desc: E330. Erosive potential. Dental studies link high intake to enamel erosion.

corn starch.desc: Highly refined carbohydrate used as a thickener. Calorie dense with zero nutrition. FDA classifies it as a processed additive.

cottonseed oil.desc: Identified as high risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

cyclamate.desc: Identified as high risk in training dataset. (Source: WHO)

degermed yellow cornmeal.desc: Corn meal with the nutritious germ removed. Lacks fiber and essential fats.

dextrose.desc: Chemically pure glucose. recognized by FDA as an added sugar that spikes insulin rapidly.

disodium guanylate.desc: E627. Flavor potentiator. Often implies high-salt ultra-processed food.

disodium inosinate.desc: E631. Synergistic enhancer. Codex approved but caution for uric acid/gout.

e124.desc: Identified as high risk in training dataset. (Source: FSSAI)

erythritol.desc: Generally safe, but a 2023 Nature Medicine study found links to increased blood clotting risk.

folic acid.desc: Identified as low risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

glycerol.desc: Humectant (E422). Safe in moderation, but FDA notes laxative effects at high levels.

high fructose corn syrup.desc: Industrial sweetener linked to fatty liver and diabetes. Research (NIH) indicates it bypasses hunger signals (leptin).

honey powder.desc: Identified as high risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

hydrogenated vegetable oil.desc: Source of Trans Fats. WHO explicitly targets global elimination of industrially produced trans fats due to heart disease risk.

ins471.desc: Identified as medium risk in training dataset. (Source: FSSAI)

ins472e.desc: Identified as medium risk in training dataset. (Source: FSSAI)

ins476.desc: Identified as medium risk in training dataset. (Source: FSSAI)

interesterified vegetable fat.desc: Chemically modified fat. Nutrition Reviews suggest it affects blood sugar similarly to trans fats.

invert sugar syrup.desc: 'Pre-digested' sugar syrup. EFSA notes rapid absorption leading to high glycemic response.

malic acid.desc: E296. Tartness regulator. High acidity affects enamel.

maltodextrin.desc: Ultra-processed thickener. Has a Glycemic Index (85-105) higher than table sugar, causing sharp insulin spikes.

milk solids.desc: Oxidized cholesterol (oxysterols) found in spray-dried milk powders.

modified palm oil.desc: Interesterified fat. Used to replace trans fats but may still negatively alter lipid metabolism.

mono- and di-glycerides of fatty acids.desc: E471. Evaluated by EFSA. While 'safe', emerging research suggests gut inflammation risks.

monosodium glutamate.desc: E621. FSSAI requires 'Contains MSG' warning. FDA recognizes it as safe but monitors 'MSG Complex'.

natural flavour.desc: FDA: 'Derived from natural source' but can be heavily processed containing solvents.

natural flavours.desc: Identified as medium risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

palmolein oil.desc: Identified as high risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

pgpr.desc: E476. Approved by Codex as chocolate emulsifier replacement for cocoa butter. Marker of ultra-processing.

pgpr (ins476).desc: Identified as medium risk in training dataset. (Source: FSSAI)

phenylalanine.desc: FSSAI/FDA require mandatory warning for Phenylketonurics.

polysorbate 80.desc: Nature study (2015) shows it erodes gut mucus barrier, leading to 'leaky gut' and colitic symptoms.

ponceau 4r.desc: Identified as high risk in training dataset. (Source: FSSAI)

potassium bromate.desc: E924a. Banned in India (FSSAI) and EU. Validated carcinogen in animal models.

potassium sorbate.desc: E202. EFSA established ADI. Considered one of the safer preservatives.

potato starch.desc: Refined starch that breaks down rapidly into glucose. Often used in ultra-processed snacks.

potatoes.desc: Identified as none risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

propyl gallate.desc: Identified as high risk in training dataset. (Source: FDA)

refined palm oil.desc: EFSA warns about process contaminants (GE/MCPD) formed during high-temp refining, which are genotoxic.

refined vegetable oil.desc: Generic solvent-extracted oils. Stripped of nutrients and prone to oxidation.

refined wheat flour.desc: Stripped of fiber and nutrients. Acts like sugar, causing rapid glucose spikes. WHO guidelines warn against high intake of refined grains.

rice.desc: Identified as none risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

saccharin.desc: Identified as medium risk in training dataset. (Source: WHO)

salt.desc: Identified as none risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

semolina.desc: Identified as medium risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

shea fat.desc: Cocoa butter equivalent. Allowed in chocolates up to 5% (EU). High saturated fat.

silicon dioxide.desc: E551. EFSA re-evaluation highlighted need to investigate nanoparticle potential.

sodium acetate.desc: Identified as medium risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

sodium benzoate.desc: E211. FDA monitors for benzene formation (carcinogen). Lancet study linked it to hyperactivity.

sodium bicarbonate.desc: E500. Baking soda. Contributes to sodium load.

sodium nitrate.desc: E251. Precursor to nitrite/nitrosamines in the body.

sodium nitrite.desc: E250. IARC classifies processed meats with nitrites as Group 1 Carcinogens.

sorbitol.desc: Polyol sweetener. EFSA warns excessive consumption causes bloating and laxative effects.

soy lecithin.desc: Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS). Source of choline. Caution only for soy allergies.

soy protein isolate.desc: Hexane-extracted. Clean Label Project often finds industrial contaminants in isolates.

soybean oil.desc: High Omega-6 linoleic acid. Studies link excess Omega-6 to systemic inflammation.

spices.desc: Identified as none risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

starch.desc: Identified as medium risk in training dataset. (Source: Open Food Facts / Common Knowledge)

sucralose.desc: Chlorinated sugar. Studies suggest it may reduce healthy gut bacteria and affect insulin sensitivity (NCBI).

sugar.desc: Free sugars. WHO recommends limiting intake to <5% of energy to prevent obesity and dental caries.

sulphur dioxide.desc: E220. FDA requires explicit labeling due to severe respiratory risks for asthmatics.

sunset yellow.desc: Yellow 6 (E110). Banned in some EU countries. FSA warns of hyperactivity risks.

tartrazine.desc: Yellow 5 (E102). FSSAI mandates labeling. Linked to hyperactivity in children (Southampton Study).

tbhq.desc: E319. NTP (National Toxicology Program) studies found higher incidence of tumors in rats.

vegetable shortening.desc: Solid fat high in pro-inflammatory omega-6s. AHA advises limiting saturated and solid fats.

xanthan gum.desc: E415. EFSA re-evaluation confirmed safety but noted laxative effect at high doses.
